data.description:
## Why

Invariant I9 says compaction must stay safe against concurrent remote appends and must
never assume the per-clone lock excludes remote writers. The existing regression
(`test_sub_horizon_append_orphan_recovered_by_fsck_rebuild` in
`tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py`) simulates the interleaving by
copying clone B's event file into clone A by hand plus a `--no-verify` commit — it never
does a real push/fetch/merge, so the actual publish/reconverge timing and retry logic
are unexercised. The report's suggested "either visible or fsck repairs it" oracle is
too permissive (a bug forcing every normal append through repair would still pass).

## What

- Add real push/fetch/merge interleaving tests with a tightened, scenario-specific
  oracle, keeping the existing manual-copy test as the fast deterministic regression.

## Scope

- `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` (or a sibling integration module)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Scenario A (normal horizon): B pushes a current-time event while A is
      mid-compaction; after A reconverges, B's event is IMMEDIATELY visible, `fsck` is
      clean, and NO repair command is required.
- [ ] Scenario B (adversarial ordering): control HLC/timestamps so B's later-published
      event sorts before A's snapshot; `fsck` deterministically flags the
      orphan/inconsistency, `--repair-snapshots` restores the exact event, both clones
      converge to identical state, and no UUID/content is lost.
- [ ] The existing manual-copy test is retained as a fast Scenario-B regression.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.
data.title: Add real remote-append-during-compaction interleaving tests
